pages.1:
# 7- Sustainability

In this section, we will first look at the environmental costs that Data Science projects can have, using examples from the literature. Then, we will share best practices, to help you transform your own data science projects into more frugal ones.

# A. About the Carbon Footprint of AI and Deep Learning

Recent advances in techniques and hardware, along with the democratization of computing resources for Data Science, have recently enabled impressive progress, both in terms of ability to put in production machine learning (ML) models and of accuracy improvements, with the use of more and more complex deep neural networks.

However, training a state-of-the-art model requires substantial computational resources that use considerable energy, associated with financial and environmental costs. Indeed, to train such models nowadays, multiple instances of specialized hardware (such as GPUs or TPUs) are needed. Research and development of new models increase the associated costs by thousands of times, because it requires iterative model retraining to experiment model architectures and hyperparameters.

# A.1. Life Cycle Assessment of common AI models

In a recent paper, researchers at the University of Massachusetts performed a life cycle assessment of training several common large AI models. More specifically, the paper focuses on the impact of large NLP models, well-known to be computationally intensive.

The picture below is a striking summary of their findings, and highlights how expensive it can be to train models in terms of carbon emissions. Indeed, the training of Transformer with 213M parameters with neural architecture search has been estimated to emit 315 times more CO2 than a roundtrip flight between New-York and San-Francisco.

Comparing the equivalent carbon emissions of training a large AI model, with different personal carbon-intensive activities - units in pounds or tons of CO2 equivalent (link)

More information from other AI models is provided in the table below. As expected, the costs increase proportionally with model size. When tuning-steps are added to the

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.2:
model, to increase its final accuracy (using neural architecture search for instance), the costs are going to the roof.

Comparison of energy (kWh), carbon (pounds of CO2equivalent) and cloud usage (USD) costs for different large AI models (link)

The carbon footprint estimation of the different models has been estimated using the following methodology. Researchers looked at 4 specific models (Transformer, ELMo, BERT and GPT-2) because these have been responsible for the biggest performance leaps in the field. They trained each model on a single GPU, for up to a day, to measure its power draw. They then used the number of training hours listed in the model's original papers to calculate the total energy consumed over the complete training process. That number was converted into pounds of carbon dioxide equivalent based on the average energy mix in the US, which closely matches the energy mix used by Amazon's AWS, the largest cloud services provider.

More details about how these estimates were computed can be found in the paper where those numbers were first presented: link.

# A.2. Can Frugal AI become a reality?

While the above analysis is rather pessimistic in terms of increased environmental impact of Machine Learning, there are ways we, at Eki and as Data Scientists, can act. Among others, the article published in 2022 by researchers from Berkeley and Google, highlights key practices that could reduce considerably the carbon footprint of Data Science products. The research team forecasts that the combination of these key practices could reduce energy consumption by 100x and cut emissions by 1000x.

Some of the key practices can only be applied by the Cloud providers. However, all actors in the Data Science world can have impact: this is what we, at Eki, call frugal AI.

For this standard, our approach relies on two important aspects for a more frugal and sustainable use of AI and Data Science 1-Measure 2-Reduce

# B. Measuring carbon footprint - A starting point to Frugal and Sustainable AI

In this section, we present two different tools that can help you monitor the carbon footprint of your projects, so that you can identify where larger savings could be made.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.3:
· Tool 1 - Carbon Footprint by Google, for measuring the carbon footprint per product, team, project for your Google Cloud Platform (GCP) projects

· Tool 2 - CodeCarbon for measuring the carbon footprint of your code

Carbon Footprint by Google works only on GCP. However, it is extremely easy to use. You will have a complete understanding of the emissions of your Data Science projects, although with lower granularity than with Code Carbon.

Code Carbon measures the carbon footprint of your code. It is very useful to compare algorithms that have a huge carbon impact but not so much for linear models that can run on a cloud function. It offers a fine granularity for the analysis of your code carbon footprint.

We recommend the use of both tools to help you monitor the carbon footprint of your projects.

B.1. Tool 1 - Carbon Footprint by Google, for measuring the carbon footprint per product, team, project for your GCP projects

## B.1.a. What is Carbon Footprint?

Carbon Footprint is a GCP native tool developed by Google that allows you to measure, report and reduce the cloud carbon emissions of the projects you run or store on GCP. More specifically, it will allow you to accurately monitor your gross cloud emissions over time by project, product, and region. Being native to GCP, no work is needed to set it up.

If the right accesses are granted, the user can directly access a dashboard built by default by Google. Thus, no time is needed to build a new dashboard and you can directly analyze the data that the tool automatically collects. A screenshot of the native dashboard is provided below.

The methodology behind the Google carbon footprint estimation has been published and is available to everyone: link.

Image from the Google Carbon Footprint documentation, showing the methodology to perform the estimation.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.4:
## B.1.b. When can I use Carbon Footprint?

It is important to note that Carbon Footprint is a tool that has been developed for measuring your carbon emissions while working on GCP services. This tool cannot be used on other Cloud providers services.

## B.1.c. Useful links related to Carbon Footprint

You may find below a list of useful links related to Carbon Footprint:

· Main page describing the tool: link

· Video and powerpoint describing the capabilities and uses of Carbon Footprint: link

· Methodology used by the tool to estimate the Carbon Footprint of your GCP projects: link

· Documentation of the tool itself: link

### B.2. Tool 2 - CodeCarbon, for measuring the carbon footprint of your code

This tool is used to measure the carbon footprint of multiple tools, and it works in the following way:

· It starts a timer when you launch your code (ie. model training, or model prediction).

· It ends the timer when your code ends (ie: at the end of the training, or prediction).

. It calculates the time, and multiplies that by an estimation of the carbon intensity where your server is located.

· It saves the result in your logs.

· The results are also aggregated in a simple user interface.

## B.2.a. Carbon consumption computation:

According to the documentation, the carbon computation is the following:

. It uses the command psutil to gather information about the cpu frequency, and power consumed.

· It uses the carbon intensity from the electricity grid your computer is linked to:

o If you are using a specific and known datacenter, it uses the official datacenter carbon intensity - see this example for GCP .

o If you are using an unknown datacenter, or from your personal computer, it uses the average carbon intensity of the location of your laptop or server (i.e. the country you set up when you first logged in).

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.5:
This second approach is a lot less precise than the first, because the carbon intensity could vary depending on where you are located, even within a country (for example in the USA).

. It then uses the formula: carbon impact = power used * time used * average carbon intensity

### B.2.b. User Interface:

The tool provides a simple interface that tells you what the carbon impact of your algorithm is equivalent to, in terms of common tools used (Driving a car, heating your house, etc ... ).

CodeCarbon user-interface is simple and flexible for adding further development ; it provides simple comparisons for raising the user awareness and making the numbers more tangible. It can be deployed to an online environment code (Cloudrun) to come.

#### B.2.c. Caveats on methodology:

· The psutil tool references a lot of processors (Intel and AMD) with their characteristics, but, event though this tool is available for Mac, Windows and Linux, most of the information is available on Linux, when the code is ran directly by the machine.

· The Log consolidation brings potential security risks, as all the logs from the different components for which you want to monitor the carbon footprint need to be monitored; it needs to be designed with security in mind, preferably at the beginning of the project, as adding CodeCarbon to an ongoing project can be tougher.

· The measure is often 0 when running simple models (LinearSVC, LogisticRegression, RandomForrest).

TLDR:

. It is very useful to compare algorithms that have a huge carbon impact (but not so much for linear models that can run on a cloud function).

. It can be used efficiently to track carbon impact of GPU hungry algorithms and centralize all the carbon impact in a dashboard.

· The tool only models CPU / GPU impact, on Kubernetes, and bare metal machines, but is not as precise on serverless architectures.

· Google Carbon Footprint would be complementary.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.6:
# C. Reducing carbon footprint and energy consumptions - A core element to ensure Frugal and Sustainable AI

In this section, we will list guidelines and best practices that we can apply in order to reduce the carbon footprint and energy consumption of our code.

## C.1. Modeling best practices

### C.1.a. One mandatory rule when beginning a machine learning project

One should always begin with simple versions of the models rather than with complex ones. They often work well and meet the needs for most projects.

### C.1.b. When complex algorithmic methods are inevitable

In some cases, the use of large and unstructured data (images, texts, videos, etc.) makes the use of algorithmic methods, such as neural networks, inevitable. In addition to the significant energy consumption that these networks can generate, learning them takes time and is expensive. Methods are now emerging to compress them in order to make them more frugal. Three of them are introduced below. Yet, those methods have low maturity levels which make them good practices to try, rather than mandatory ones. Indeed, the impact they will have on the efficiency of the models will depend on the specific use case.

One of them is known as "Pruning" and consists, as its name suggests, in cutting off certain branches of the neural network, in order to keep only the information that is really necessary for solving the "use case" at the expense of a slight loss of performance. The model becomes less heavy, trains faster and consumes less power.

Figure highlighting before and after the pruning data compression method. Source: Wikipedia (link)

With "Quantization", we simplify the information that circulates in the neurons, by rounding off the numerical values, and thus lighten the weight of the algorithm.

Finally, a last method, the "Distillation", is the one that is sometimes highlighted as the one returning the best results so far. In simple terms, it consists of training the complex and energy-intensive AI algorithm first, then letting it transfer this initial

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.7:
training to a distilled or "student" model, which is less powerful and therefore less resource-intensive, so that it can continue the work. By using this method, we manage to reduce the number of parameters and the impact of the algorithm significantly, while preserving a nice consistency with the results. For example, the DistillBERT algorithm, a distilled version of Google AI's BERT algorithm created by Hugging Face, uses 40% fewer parameters, trains 60% faster, and retains 95% of the original algorithm's performance.

The generic teacher-student framework for knowledge distillation. Source: Arxiv (link)

### C.2. Data Handling best practices

Here, we list best practices and guidelines about how best to store, manipulate and process your data to minimize its carbon footprint.

### C.2.a. Use the most pertinent type of storage

Different types of storage exist, with different characteristics. They vary slightly between one provider to another, but usually range from Hot / Standard Storage to Archive Storage, passing by Cold Storage. Let's take the case of GCP storages. Here is the definition of the 4 types of storages for GCP:

· Standard Storage: Good for "hot" data that's accessed frequently, including websites, streaming videos, and mobile apps.

· Nearline Storage: Low cost. Good for data that can be stored for at least 30 days, including data backup and long-tail multimedia content.

· Coldline Storage: Very low cost. Good for data that can be stored for at least 90 days, including disaster recovery.

· Archive Storage: Lowest cost. Good for data that can be stored for at least 365 days, including regulatory archives.

These storage types have different storage carbon emissions and it is thus very important to often question the pertinence of the storage you use for your data. One question to have in mind: do I really need such an expensive storage, or could a more "cold" one be enough for the uses I have for my data?

### C.2.b. Do not store unused files

Put the unused data / files in the archive storage as soon as they are no longer needed.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.8:
Example: In the case where you continuously receive data, for many years, some of it won't be used for any analysis as it is too old: always keep in mind to compress it and store it in archive.

### C.2.c. Compress your files

Compress your files whenever possible to take the least possible space. Both in the storage and in the transit.

Example: In the case where you continuously receive data, for many years, some of it won't be used for any analysis as it is too old: always keep in mind to compress it and store it in archive.

Parquet Format - A great format for storing data

Parquet format is very useful to store data in Data Lakes for instance as it can be compressed and partitionned, but these options have to be well set to improve transformations and storage.

Choose the good compression type

· Snappy is the compression type with the best performance

· Gzip where the data will be the most compressed

Choose the good columns for the partition

· Recommended size for a partition is 1GB

· The columns and their order are essential as the data is first partitionned by the first column, then the second ...

Regarding Virtual Machines / Clusters

The practices listed here are pertinent whatever the Cloud provider. However, the right name will change depending on the service / cloud provider used. We will use here the name cluster for something that may also for instance cover GCP virtual machines.

· Stop the cluster when it is not used

o Always set up the time of inactivity before automatically stopping the cluster. For most services, this feature is available

· Prefer cluster with multiple small machines than few big ones

o With an adaptative cluster, only the needed machines will be started

o smaller machines allow to be more precise on the resources

· Don't hesitate to create different clusters for different usage

o If you are doing both data engineering and data analysis or exploration, it can be a good idea to create different clusters to fit optimally the needs. Another example: if you plan to train a big algorithm needing

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.9:
GPU, no need to use the same machine you plan to use for training for data exploration.

· Use the smallest dataset needed for development/testing

o To need less resources and start less machines, for a shorter time

## C.3. Cloud Infrastructure best practices

This page is largely inspired from Google best practices regarding how to reduce your Cloud carbon footprint making clever choices regarding Cloud infrastructure. In this standard, only the key elements are highlighted. For detailed information and advice, you may access Google best practices on this page: link

Although those best practices were written by Google and relate to Google solutions, they are pertinent whatever the Cloud provider for your Data Science project.

## C.3.a. Choose the most suitable cloud regions

· If your project is running on GCP, use the Google Cloud Region Picker to determine the best cloud regions according to carbon footprint, price, and latency as relative considerations. Otherwise, take into consideration documentation provided by your Cloud provider regarding carbon footprint to orientate your choices of cloud regions.

· Consider creating organizational policies to restrict usage to Low CO2 regions. For GCP, documentation creating organization policies to restrict resource locations can be found here: link.

Choose the most suitable cloud services

· Migrate VMs from less efficient on-premises data centers to Compute Engine for GCP, Amazon EC2 or Azure Virtual Machines. Documentation regarding migration on GCP compute engine can be found here: link

· Use cloud managed services that are optimized for specific workloads instead of self-managed VMs.

Minimize idle cloud resources

· On GCP, use Active Assist features to delete unused VMs, optimize VM shapes, and shut down unattended projects.

· Identify opportunities to create (and delete) cloud resources as they're needed, instead of keeping them perpetually.

· Schedule VMs to start and stop according to when they're needed. Related GCP documentation can be found here: link

· Migrate workloads to containers, and run them efficiently using managed services such as Cloud Run and GKE. Related GCP documentation can be found here: link

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.10:
· Refactor monolithic applications to microservices to gain efficiency benefits. Related GCP documentation can be found here: link

· Use services that decouple compute and storage for data processing and analytics.

· Migrate existing VM workloads to managed services.

Reduce emissions for batch workloads

. Run batch workloads that have minimal or no cross-region egress in low CO2 regions.

· Run batch workloads during times of day coinciding with lower grid carbon intensity where possible.

Configuration choice for your machine

· When beginning a project, one should always work first with machine using only CPU, and change for machine equipped with GPU or TPU when there is a real need for it.

### Interesting links and tools

· Link to the Google documentation that inspired this Confluence page: link

· Link to the "Region Picker" GCP native tool. This tool helps you pick a Google Cloud region considering carbon footprint, price and latency: link

· Link to the "Active Assist" GCP native tool. This tool provides automated recommendations to optimize your cloud usage: link

· Link to the list of Google Cloud Region with carbon footprint indications: link

<!-- PageBreak -->

## C.3.a. Choose the most suitable cloud regions

· If your project is running on GCP, use the Google Cloud Region Picker to determine the best cloud regions according to carbon footprint, price, and latency as relative considerations. Otherwise, take into consideration documentation provided by your Cloud provider regarding carbon footprint to orientate your choices of cloud regions.

· Consider creating organizational policies to restrict usage to Low CO2 regions. For GCP, documentation creating organization policies to restrict resource locations can be found here: link.

Choose the most suitable cloud services

· Migrate VMs from less efficient on-premises data centers to Compute Engine for GCP, Amazon EC2 or Azure Virtual Machines. Documentation regarding migration on GCP compute engine can be found here: link

· Use cloud managed services that are optimized for specific workloads instead of self-managed VMs.

Minimize idle cloud resources

· On GCP, use Active Assist features to delete unused VMs, optimize VM shapes, and shut down unattended projects.

· Identify opportunities to create (and delete) cloud resources as they're needed, instead of keeping them perpetually.

· Schedule VMs to start and stop according to when they're needed. Related GCP documentation can be found here: link

· Migrate workloads to containers, and run them efficiently using managed services such as Cloud Run and GKE. Related GCP documentation can be found here: link

<!-- PageBreak -->

· Refactor monolithic applications to microservices to gain efficiency benefits. Related GCP documentation can be found here: link

· Use services that decouple compute and storage for data processing and analytics.

· Migrate existing VM workloads to managed services.

Reduce emissions for batch workloads

. Run batch workloads that have minimal or no cross-region egress in low CO2 regions.

· Run batch workloads during times of day coinciding with lower grid carbon intensity where possible.

Configuration choice for your machine

· When beginning a project, one should always work first with machine using only CPU, and change for machine equipped with GPU or TPU when there is a real need for it.

### Interesting links and tools

· Link to the Google documentation that inspired this Confluence page: link

· Link to the "Region Picker" GCP native tool. This tool helps you pick a Google Cloud region considering carbon footprint, price and latency: link

· Link to the "Active Assist" GCP native tool. This tool provides automated recommendations to optimize your cloud usage: link

· Link to the list of Google Cloud Region with carbon footprint indications: link

<!-- PageBreak -->
